shortDescription.text: Server-Side Request Forgery via user-controlled API tool schema URLs
SSRF via remote schema URL fetch endpoint
Arbitrary OpenAPI schema injection allows defining tool endpoints pointing to internal services
Registered custom API tools can exfiltrate data to attacker-controlled servers during workflow execution

message.text: The POST /console/api/workspaces/current/tool-provider/api/add endpoint accepts a user-supplied OpenAPI schema (payload.schema_) that defines arbitrary server URLs. These URLs are stored and later used by the system to make HTTP requests during workflow tool invocation. An authenticated admin/owner can register an API tool pointing to internal network addresses (e.g., http://169.254.169.254, http://localhost:8080) enabling SSRF attacks against cloud metadata services or internal infrastructure. The schema field is parsed by ApiBasedToolSchemaParser.auto_parse_to_tool_bundle() but no URL allowlist or blocklist validation is applied to the server URLs defined within the schema.

The GET /console/api/workspaces/current/tool-provider/api/remote endpoint in ToolApiProviderGetRemoteSchemaApi fetches a remote URL (query.url) server-side via httpx.get() in get_api_tool_provider_remote_schema(). Although pydantic HttpUrl validation constrains the URL format, there is no SSRF protection against internal/private IP ranges. An attacker can supply URLs targeting internal services (http://10.0.0.1, http://metadata.google.internal, http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/). The comment 'try to parse schema, avoid SSRF attack' in the service layer only validates the response is valid schema - it does not validate the target URL against internal networks.

The create_api_tool_provider() method in ApiToolManageService stores user-supplied OpenAPI/Swagger schema directly into the database (db_provider.schema = schema) after parsing it into tool bundles. The schema can define arbitrary API endpoint paths, HTTP methods, headers, and parameters. When these tools are later invoked during workflow execution, the system will construct and send HTTP requests based on these attacker-controlled definitions. There is no validation that the defined endpoints are safe or that the schema doesn't define operations that could be abused (e.g., PUT/DELETE methods against internal services).

An authenticated admin/owner can register a custom API tool provider that points to an attacker-controlled external server. When this tool is subsequently used in AI workflows or agent executions, conversation data, user inputs, and intermediate results can be sent as HTTP request parameters/body to the external server. The endpoint requires admin/owner role (@is_admin_or_owner_required) but a compromised admin account or malicious insider can leverage this for persistent data exfiltration. The credentials (including API keys) are encrypted before storage, but the tool invocation itself sends workflow data to the configured external endpoint.
